text:
# Button Cell Inspection Profile Reference

## Defect pattern
Button-cell inspections can show localized score excursions even when the part is ultimately normal. Compact anomalies should therefore be interpreted against trusted baseline handling notes before escalation.

## Sensor signature
Cross-modal inconsistency may appear in compact regions because of reflective surfaces and local baseline variation. Treat global descriptor drift cautiously and confirm whether the region corresponds to a benign normal-profile artifact.

## Likely root causes
- Reflective variation on the metal surface
- Small alignment or lighting mismatch in the captured view
- Benign local contrast change that does not indicate true damage

## Inspection checks
- Compare the dominant region against a normal button-cell reference
- Confirm whether the highlighted area corresponds to a known reflective or alignment artifact
- Escalate only if the compact anomaly is accompanied by clear physical damage
title: Button Cell Inspection Profile Reference

text:
## Corrective action
If physical damage is not visible, retain the sample as a likely normal case and continue monitoring. If the same compact region repeatedly escalates, review lighting, placement, and baseline normalization.

## Trust notes
This reference is appropriate for button-cell inspections where compact detector signals require normal-profile verification rather than immediate rejection.
title: Button Cell Inspection Profile Reference

text:
# Capsule External Damage Reference

## Defect pattern
Capsule shell damage or broken outside surfaces often produce a compact anomaly with strong detector disagreement and clear cross-modal inconsistency. In challenging cases, the internal-defect rescue pathway may also flag the sample.

## Sensor signature
Prioritize compact regions where appearance disagreement is high but local thermal and geometric support are weak or mixed. Cross-modal inconsistency is especially important for capsule faults that do not present as obvious broad surface damage.

## Likely root causes
- Outer shell fracture from handling impact
- Seal deformation after improper assembly pressure
- Internal structure shift creating inconsistent exterior cues

## Inspection checks
- Examine the compact region for shell cracks or edge breakage
- Check whether neighboring shell geometry remains consistent with a normal capsule
- Confirm whether internal-defect cues are present despite weak broad surface support

## Corrective action
Escalate capsule samples with compact cross-modal inconsistency for manual shell and seal inspection. Review impact handling and assembly pressure history if the same fault repeats.
title: Capsule External Damage Reference

text:
## Trust notes
This reference is appropriate when the detector reports capsule anomalies with compact cross-modal inconsistency and elevated global descriptor drift.
title: Capsule External Damage Reference

text:
# Cotton Inspection Profile Reference

## Defect pattern
Cotton samples can occasionally trigger compact detector responses even when the part remains acceptable. These cases should be checked against the normal texture profile before escalation.

## Sensor signature
Compact cross-modal inconsistency with weak thermal support can occur when the surface texture varies locally. Treat global descriptor drift as a verification cue, not an automatic reject signal.

## Likely causes
- Benign local texture variation
- Small placement or lighting mismatch during capture
- Minor surface irregularity that does not indicate functional damage

## Inspection checks
- Compare the highlighted region against a known-good cotton reference
- Confirm whether the compact region reflects normal texture variation rather than a true defect
- Escalate only if the region grows, repeats, or shows obvious material damage

## Corrective action
Retain as monitored-normal if the region matches expected texture variation. Review capture alignment or baseline normalization if the same cue appears repeatedly.
title: Cotton Inspection Profile Reference

text:
## Trust notes
Use this profile when cotton inspections show compact detector activity without strong physical evidence of damage.
title: Cotton Inspection Profile Reference

text:
# Cube Hole and Missing Material Reference

## Defect pattern
Cube hole defects usually present as compact missing-material anomalies. The detector often shows high global drift because a local cavity changes the expected object profile significantly.

## Sensor signature
Expect compact residual disagreement with cross-modal inconsistency around the missing-material region. Geometry may appear weaker than expected locally, so compare the region against known cube continuity.

## Likely root causes
- Missing material after molding or machining
- Local punch-through or cavity defect
- Edge loss caused by handling damage

## Inspection checks
- Inspect the dominant region for true material absence
- Compare local faces and edges against a normal cube
- Verify whether the cavity is internal, through-hole, or edge-adjacent

## Corrective action
Reject the part, inspect the forming process for incomplete fill or punch-through, and review whether repeated holes appear at the same face location.

## Trust notes
Use this guide when the detector highlights a compact cube anomaly with strong descriptor drift and cross-modal inconsistency.
title: Cube Hole and Missing Material Reference

text:
# Flat Pad Break Reference

## Defect pattern
Broken flat-pad samples tend to concentrate in a compact region while the detector reports strong global descriptor drift. The anomaly may remain visually subtle but still break the learned normal pad profile.

## Sensor signature
Expect compact cross-modal inconsistency with weak local thermal and geometric support. Confirm the region against pad edge continuity and local material breakage.

## Likely root causes
- Edge fracture during handling
- Local material tear or chip-out
- Compression damage that changed the expected outline

## Inspection checks
- Inspect the compact region for chipped edges or broken corners
- Compare the pad outline with a known normal part
- Check whether the affected area matches a stress concentration point

## Corrective action
Remove the damaged pad, verify fixture pressure and handling contact points, and review whether breakage clusters near a repeated edge location.

## Trust notes
This reference supports compact flat-pad anomalies dominated by detector residuals and global descriptor drift.
title: Flat Pad Break Reference

text:
# Light Housing Break Reference

## Defect pattern
Broken light samples usually appear as compact localized faults with strong global drift and mixed cross-modal evidence. The detector often treats the region as a high-severity compact anomaly.

## Sensor signature
Look for a tight region with strong residual disagreement and cross-modal inconsistency. Geometry or thermal support may be weak locally, so confirm the anomaly against housing continuity and known part silhouette.

## Likely root causes
- Cracked housing after impact
- Broken edge near lens or enclosure seam
- Local misfit introduced during handling or packaging

## Inspection checks
- Inspect the housing edge around the dominant region
- Compare enclosure outline with a known-good part
- Check for chipped or displaced material at the break boundary

## Corrective action
Remove the unit from outbound handling, verify enclosure integrity, and review impact or packaging events if multiple lights fail in the same pattern.

## Trust notes
This reference is suited to compact light anomalies where the detector emphasizes score-basis evidence and global descriptor drift.
title: Light Housing Break Reference

text:
# Nut Scratch and Surface Damage Reference

## Defect pattern
Scratched nut samples often appear as compact anomalies with strong residual disagreement and moderate descriptor drift. The fault may be shallow but still disrupt the normal surface profile.

## Sensor signature
Look for a compact region with mixed cross-modal support. Thermal evidence is typically weak, so the best confirmation comes from local surface deviation and the residual map.

## Likely root causes
- Abrasion from hard contact during transport
- Tool slippage during tightening or placement
- Foreign particles scoring the surface

## Inspection checks
- Inspect the highlighted region for scratch lines or gouges
- Compare the damaged surface to a known-good nut
- Review whether the scratch aligns with tooling or contact direction

## Corrective action
Segregate the part, inspect contact tooling and feeders, and clean any abrasive contamination from the process path.

## Trust notes
This reference supports compact nut scratches where the detector shows cross-modal inconsistency and elevated descriptor drift.
title: Nut Scratch and Surface Damage Reference

text:
# Piggy Color Shift Reference

## Defect pattern
Piggy color anomalies often behave as compact appearance faults with strong score-basis disagreement and descriptor drift. Local geometry may remain weakly affected while the overall appearance profile shifts away from normal.

## Sensor signature
Look for a compact region with cross-modal inconsistency and strong residual response. Thermal evidence is often weak, so confirmation depends on localized appearance mismatch and deviation from the learned global profile.

## Likely root causes
- Pigment inconsistency during finishing
- Surface contamination or coating mismatch
- Local discoloration after thermal or chemical exposure

## Inspection checks
- Inspect the highlighted region for color or tone deviation
- Compare the surface finish against a known-good piggy sample
- Check whether the anomaly is localized or spreading beyond the compact region

## Corrective action
Separate the part for finish review, inspect pigment or coating controls, and look for contamination or exposure events in the upstream process.
title: Piggy Color Shift Reference

text:
## Trust notes
This reference is intended for compact piggy appearance anomalies dominated by score-basis evidence and global descriptor drift.
title: Piggy Color Shift Reference

text:
# Plastic Cylinder Inspection Profile Reference

## Defect pattern
Plastic-cylinder inspections may produce compact detector highlights on otherwise normal parts when shape alignment or local appearance varies within the expected range.

## Sensor signature
Look for compact cross-modal inconsistency with weak thermal and geometric support. If the anomaly does not correspond to visible material damage, treat it as a normal-profile verification case first.

## Likely causes
- Benign capture misalignment
- Normal reflective or contour variation
- Small local contrast shift without true structural damage

## Inspection checks
- Compare the highlighted region with a known normal plastic cylinder
- Confirm that the compact cue does not correspond to a crack, hole, or missing material
- Review whether the same region recurs across multiple normal samples

## Corrective action
Keep the sample as monitored-normal if no physical damage is visible. Review placement and baseline normalization if the same compact cue appears frequently.

## Trust notes
This profile is intended for detector signals that require a normal-reference comparison before escalation.
title: Plastic Cylinder Inspection Profile Reference

text:
# Screen Scratch Inspection Reference

## Defect pattern
Screen scratches commonly present as compact or narrow anomalies with clear appearance disagreement. Thermal support is often weak, so the detector relies on residual structure and descriptor drift.

## Sensor signature
Focus on compact regions with strong score-basis response and cross-modal inconsistency. Geometry may not dominate, but a scratched screen can still shift the global descriptor distribution away from the normal profile.

## Likely root causes
- Surface abrasion during transport
- Contact with hard tooling or foreign particles
- Cleaning or handling damage on the viewing surface

## Inspection checks
- Inspect the highlighted region under angled lighting
- Verify whether the anomaly aligns with a scratch line or surface abrasion patch
- Compare local surface continuity with a known normal screen

## Corrective action
Segregate scratched units, inspect cleaning and handling stations, and review contact surfaces for abrasive contaminants.

## Trust notes
Use this guide when screen samples are flagged with compact score-basis anomalies and cross-modal inconsistency.
title: Screen Scratch Inspection Reference

text:
# Screw Break Geometry Reference

## Defect pattern
Broken screw samples usually appear as compact structural disruptions with strong descriptor drift relative to the normal thread profile. The detector often emphasizes compact residual disagreement plus geometry-sensitive deviation.

## Sensor signature
Expect cross-modal inconsistency with a tight anomalous region around the damaged thread or broken shaft area. Thermal evidence may remain weak while the global descriptor branch reinforces the anomaly.

## Likely root causes
- Excess fastening torque or mechanical overload
- Material fatigue at the thread root
- Local shaft fracture after repeated stress cycles

## Inspection checks
- Confirm whether the dominant region lies on the thread path or shaft edge
- Review thread continuity against a known-good screw
- Check for brittle fracture signs or chipped edges

## Corrective action
Remove the defective screw from the line, inspect fastening torque settings, and review whether the break location is consistent with thread-root overstress or handling impact.
title: Screw Break Geometry Reference

text:
## Trust notes
Use this guide when the detector highlights a compact screw anomaly with strong global drift and mixed cross-modal evidence.
title: Screw Break Geometry Reference

text:
# Solar Panel Thermal Profile Reference

## Defect pattern
Solar-panel inspections may activate internal thermal cues even for acceptable baseline samples. The key distinction is whether the suspected region stays compact and consistent with known benign thermal structure.

## Sensor signature
When thermal-internal cues are present, compare hotspot area, compactness, and mean hotspot intensity against the normal panel profile. Cross-modal inconsistency alone is not enough to confirm a true defect.

## Likely root causes
- Benign thermal concentration around expected conductive paths
- Local measurement variation near connectors or panel features
- True internal heating only if the compact cue expands or intensifies abnormally

## Inspection checks
- Compare the suspected region against a trusted normal thermal reference
- Verify whether hotspot compactness remains within the expected pattern
- Escalate if the region broadens, intensifies, or repeats outside the known baseline zone
title: Solar Panel Thermal Profile Reference

text:
## Corrective action
Keep the sample under monitored review if the signature matches the benign baseline. Escalate for thermal reinspection if internal-heating cues intensify or spread beyond the expected compact region.

## Trust notes
Use this reference when solar-panel detections emphasize internal thermal cues and cross-modal inconsistency but still require confirmation against the normal baseline.
title: Solar Panel Thermal Profile Reference

text:
# Spring Pad Break Reference

## Defect pattern
Spring-pad fractures commonly appear as compact anomalies with strong descriptor drift away from the normal spring profile. The detector often assigns high severity even when thermal support is weak.

## Sensor signature
Watch for compact cross-modal inconsistency near the spring edge or loaded contact area. Compare the dominant region against the expected spring contour and compression geometry.

## Likely root causes
- Fatigue fracture after repeated compression
- Edge damage from misaligned insertion
- Local break after excessive load

## Inspection checks
- Inspect the highlighted region for cracked spring material
- Compare contour continuity with a known-good spring pad
- Verify whether the defect sits near a compression hot spot

## Corrective action
Quarantine the part, review insertion alignment and compression force, and inspect upstream tooling for repeated localized overload.

## Trust notes
This reference is intended for compact spring-pad break detections with elevated global descriptor drift.
title: Spring Pad Break Reference

text:
# Toothbrush Break Reference

## Defect pattern
Broken toothbrush parts usually manifest as compact structural anomalies with strong deviation from the normal shape profile. The dominant detector evidence often comes from residual mismatch and global drift.

## Sensor signature
Look for compact cross-modal inconsistency around the broken edge or head attachment area. Thermal evidence may remain weak, so visual-geometry agreement is the main check.

## Likely root causes
- Impact damage during handling
- Weak bonding at the head or handle junction
- Local plastic fracture under bending stress

## Inspection checks
- Inspect the compact region for edge separation or chipped plastic
- Compare the handle-head outline with a known-good toothbrush
- Check whether the break aligns with a repeated stress point

## Corrective action
Reject the damaged part, inspect the junction or high-stress region, and review handling or bend-load conditions if repeated faults appear.

## Trust notes
Use this guide when the detector identifies compact toothbrush anomalies with strong score-basis evidence and cross-modal inconsistency.
title: Toothbrush Break Reference

text:
# Zipper Crack and Separation Field Guide

## Defect pattern
Cracks around zipper teeth or seam attachment points usually appear as compact structural breaks. In shared inspection, these faults often surface as strong residual disagreement with moderate geometric irregularity.

## Sensor signature
Look for cross-modal inconsistency around a compact region. Geometry should be checked for local tooth displacement, edge separation, or seam misalignment even when thermal evidence is weak.

## Likely root causes
- Mechanical overstress during stitching or attachment
- Repeated bending that weakens the zipper tooth base
- Local seam pull-out or tooth misalignment

## Inspection checks
- Verify tooth continuity and edge alignment
- Check whether the compact anomaly sits near the seam or tooth root
- Compare the dominant region against known straight-line zipper geometry

## Corrective action
If the crack is localized, isolate the defective zipper segment and inspect upstream attachment tooling. If repeated samples show the same compact seam-adjacent fault, review stitching force and alignment setup.
title: Zipper Crack and Separation Field Guide

text:
## Trust notes
This reference is intended for detector-grounded follow-up on zipper crack findings where cross-modal inconsistency and geometric change dominate the evidence.
title: Zipper Crack and Separation Field Guide
